card.intuition: For each position i, compute the length of the longest substring starting at i that is also a prefix of the whole string — this Z-array enables O(n) pattern matching.
card.analogy: You have a secret codeword and you're scanning a long message. At each position, you measure: 'how long does this segment match the beginning of my codeword?' The Z-array records those lengths.
card.time_complexity: O(n) — the Z-box window [l, r] only ever moves right
when_to_use: Pattern matching: search for pattern P in text T by running Z on P+'$'+T and finding Z[i] == len(P)
Finding all positions where a string is a rotation of another
Counting distinct substrings or checking period of a string
When you need the Z-array as a building block for suffix automaton construction
gotchas: Z[0] is defined as the length of the entire string (the whole string matches its own prefix) — some implementations set it to 0 conventionally
The Z-box [l, r] is the interval of the rightmost Z-box seen so far — when i falls inside it, z[i] ≥ min(r-i+1, z[i-l]) before extending
Use a separator character (e.g., '$') not appearing in either string when concatenating pattern and text for search

exercise.prompt: Complete the Z-function. For each index i, z[i] = length of the longest prefix of s that matches s[i:].
hints: When z[i] extends beyond the current right edge r, the new Z-box starts at i and ends at i + z[i]
Update l = i and r = i + z[i] — the right boundary is exclusive (one past the last matching character)
exercise.test_code:
z = z_function('aabxaa')
assert z[0] == 6,        'z[0] always equals len(s)'
assert z[1] == 1,        'z[1]: "abxaa" matches "a" at start'
assert z[2] == 0,        'z[2]: "bxaa" no prefix match'
assert z[4] == 2,        'z[4]: "aa" matches prefix "aa"'

z2 = z_function('aaaa')
assert z2 == [4,3,2,1],  'All-same string: z[i] = n-i'

z3 = z_function('abcabc')
assert z3[3] == 3,       'z[3]: "abc" matches 3-char prefix'
print('PASS')
